inlineContent.text: Dictation can be used to enter text by speaking instead of typing. This can be complicated to set up, since keyboard extensions are not allowed to access the microphone. KeyboardKit makes this easy.
👑 Dictation requires a KeyboardKit Pro license with
support, unless you want to implement the feature from scratch. You can sign up for KeyboardKit Pro from the
developer guide for detailed information on how to set up dictation for a main application and its keyboard extension.
class with observable state and auto-persisted dictation
, that can be used to get the currently dictated text and customize the dictation behavior.
KeyboardKit automatically creates an instance of this class and registers it with its
, and also injects it into the view environment.

-based dictation. App-based dictation opens the main app to perform dictation, after which it tries to return to the keyboard to apply the result, while keyboard-based dictation is performed within the keyboard.
Keyboard-based dictation requires that you enable background audio to keep the main app open in the background. Due to this, app-based dictation is the default dictation method.
can be used to perform dictation from both an app or a keyboard extension. The keyboard can open the main app to start dictation, then navigate back to perform dictation from within the extension.
until you set up KeyboardKit Pro or inject your own service implementation.
type with auto-persisted settings that can be used to customize the dictation behavior.
is used by default. KeyboardKit Pro unlocks a feature-specific
that can be used to handle these settings.
KeyboardKit Pro unlocks a standard dictation service, a speech recognizer, as well as a bunch of views and dictation-specific utilities.
These services and utilities require a KeyboardKit Pro license that unlocks the
that can be used trigger dictation from a keyboard extension, by opening the main app to start dictation, then (if possible) return to the keyboard to apply the result.
KeyboardKit Pro will automatically register a standard dictation service as the main
requires that you pass in a
developer guide has source code for a local, private, on-device speech recognizer that supports the following locales:

Arabic (ar-SA), Cantonese (yue-CN), Catalan (ca-ES), Chinese (zh-CN), Chinese (zh-HK), Chinese (zh-TW), Croatian (hr-HR), Czech (cs-CZ), Danish (da-DK), Dutch (nl-BE), Dutch (nl-NL), English (en-AE), English (en-AU), English (en-CA), English (en-GB), English (en-ID), English (en-IE), English (en-IN), English (en-NZ), English (en-PH), English (en-SA), English (en-SG), English (en-US), English (en-ZA), Finnish (fi-FI), French (fr-BE), French (fr-CA), French (fr-CH), French (fr-FR), German (de-AT), German (de-CH), German (de-DE), Greek (el-GR), Hebrew (he-IL), Hindi (hi-IN), Hindi (hi-IN-translit), Hindi (hi-Latn), Hungarian (hu-HU), Indonesian (id-ID), Italian (it-CH), Italian (it-IT), Japanese (ja-JP), Korean (ko-KR), Malay (ms-MY), Norwegian Bokmål (nb-NO), Polish (pl-PL), Portuguese (pt-BR), Portuguese (pt-PT), Romanian (ro-RO), Russian (ru-RU), Shanghainese (wuu-CN), Slovak (sk-SK), Spanish (es-419), Spanish (es-CL), Spanish (es-CO), Spanish (es-ES), Spanish (es-MX), Spanish (es-US), Swedish (sv-SE), Thai (th-TH), Turkish (tr-TR), Ukrainian (uk-UA), Vietnamese (vi-VN)
You can implement the speech recognizer protocol from scratch, for instance to use your own
to record the microphone volume during dictation. The volume levels can be visualized with a
